findings.summary: StageResult is defined independently in both checkpoint.py and pipeline_reporter.py with overlapping but different fields
evidence: checkpoint.py:22-32 defines StageResult with fields: name, exit_code, duration, timestamp, completed, status
pipeline_reporter.py:23-42 defines a separate StageResult with fields: name, exit_code, duration, status, output_files, errors, warnings

pipeline_reporter.py imports MultiProjectResult from infrastructure.core.multi_project via TYPE_CHECKING, which itself uses PipelineStageResult from infrastructure.core.pipeline — three coexisting stage result types with overlapping semantics
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into a single StageResult in infrastructure/core/pipeline.py or a shared models module, and have the reporting layer extend or wrap it rather than redefine it

findings.summary: pipeline_reporter.py imports MultiProjectResult from core under TYPE_CHECKING but then calls getattr on live result objects, making the dependency real but invisible to static analysis
evidence: pipeline_reporter.py:17-18: 'if TYPE_CHECKING: from infrastructure.core.multi_project import MultiProjectResult'
pipeline_reporter.py:98-99: accesses result.successful_projects, result.failed_projects, result.total_duration, result.project_results at runtime — all live attribute reads on the imported type
findings.suggestion: Either import MultiProjectResult unconditionally (it is a genuine runtime dependency) or define a narrower Protocol that captures only the attributes the reporter needs, placed in the reporting boundary

findings.summary: coverage_parser.py mixes returning None, returning False/None tuples, and raising exceptions across its parsing helpers with no consistent contract
evidence: coverage_parser.py:257-273: _parse_coverage_json returns Optional[float] (None on failure)
coverage_parser.py:276-309: extract_coverage_percentage returns tuple[bool, Optional[float]]
coverage_parser.py:193-230: check_test_failures returns tuple[bool, str]
coverage_parser.py:41-70: _parse_failures_section returns list[dict] (empty list on no match)
Four different failure-signalling conventions within a single 310-line module: Optional return, (bool, Optional) tuple, (bool, str) tuple, and empty-list sentinel
findings.suggestion: Choose one failure convention for the module boundary — (bool, value) tuples are already used by the public API; align the private helpers to return empty collections consistently rather than None

findings.summary: PerformanceMonitor._get_memory_usage and _get_cpu_percent catch bare Exception and return 0.0 silently, masking genuine psutil failures
evidence: performance.py:136-139: except Exception: return 0.0 in _get_memory_usage — no log, no propagation
performance.py:144-148: except Exception: return 0.0 in _get_cpu_percent — no log, no propagation
health_check.py:62-69 by contrast logs warnings for every failed check, showing an inconsistent standard within the same infrastructure/core/ layer
findings.suggestion: At minimum log a debug-level warning so failures surface during diagnosis; if psutil attribute errors are expected, catch specifically AttributeError or OSError as done in StagePerformanceTracker.end_stage (performance.py:251)

findings.summary: run_test_summary_generation writes JSON and markdown with bare open() calls that will raise OSError on disk/permission failure but other file reads in the same module are wrapped in try/except
evidence: test_summary_generator.py:370-371: bare 'with open(json_file, "w") as f:' — no exception handling
test_summary_generator.py:376-378: bare 'with open(md_file, "w") as f:' — no exception handling
test_summary_generator.py:31-42: load_test_results wraps open() in try/except (OSError, json.JSONDecodeError)
test_summary_generator.py:58-79: load_infrastructure_results wraps open() in try/except
findings.suggestion: Wrap the write calls in the same OSError handler pattern used for reads, or let the exception propagate explicitly with a typed raise rather than a silent crash

findings.summary: Several scripts in scripts/ omit log_header and log_success while 14/18 and 12/18 siblings respectively use them, creating inconsistent pipeline output
evidence: Blind packet conventions data: scripts/ shared_patterns shows log_header used in 14/18 files, log_success in 12/18 files
scripts/02_run_analysis.py is listed as missing get_logger, log_header, log_success — yet it is a substantive pipeline stage script comparable to siblings that carry all three
scripts/execute_pipeline.py and scripts/generate_pipeline_reports.py missing log_header and log_success despite being primary pipeline orchestrators
findings.suggestion: Adopt log_header at entry and log_success at exit uniformly across all substantive pipeline stage scripts to match the established pattern

findings.summary: health_check.py exposes three module-level wrapper functions (quick_health_check, get_health_status, get_health_metrics) that do nothing except forward to a lazily created module-level singleton, adding an indirection layer with no policy
evidence: health_check.py:291-296: _get_checker() creates and caches SystemHealthChecker in a module global
health_check.py:299-301: quick_health_check() = _get_checker().is_healthy() — pure forward
health_check.py:304-306: get_health_status() = _get_checker().get_health_status() — pure forward
health_check.py:309-311: get_health_metrics() = _get_checker().get_metrics() — pure forward
performance.py:516-521 follows the same singleton pattern with get_performance_monitor() -> CodeProfiler
findings.suggestion: Either expose the class directly and let callers instantiate it, or use a module-level instance without the intermediate _get_checker indirection. The pattern is repeated across at least two modules suggesting a structural habit rather than a deliberate design

findings.summary: reportlab declared as core dependency but only used behind optional/lazy guards
evidence: pyproject.toml line 43: 'reportlab>=4.0.0' listed under [project].dependencies (always installed)
Only 3 infrastructure files import reportlab, all in optional/specialized subsystems (steganography, manuscript overview)

psutil is in optional 'monitoring' group yet is directly imported (with try/except guard) in performance.py, health_check.py, logging_utils.py — the guard is correct but the group placement signals optionality that is contradicted by widespread production use
findings.suggestion: Move reportlab to an optional dependency group (e.g., 'rendering') with a lazy import guard matching the steganography and monitoring patterns already used. Alternatively, promote psutil to core dependencies to match its pervasive guarded use across 5 production modules.

findings.summary: Every pass-only exception subclass carries a multi-line docstring with Examples that restate the class name
evidence: exceptions.py line 75-91: ConfigurationError body is 'pass', preceded by a 9-line docstring listing what kinds of errors it covers and a full >>> raise example
exceptions.py line 94-104: MissingConfigurationError body is 'pass', with its own 5-line docstring and code example
exceptions.py line 107-117: InvalidConfigurationError body is 'pass', same pattern
exceptions.py line 125-141: ValidationError body is 'pass', 11-line docstring with bullet list and example
Pattern repeats for MarkdownValidationError, PDFValidationError, DataValidationError, BuildError — all pass-only bodies with multi-line docstrings
The docstring content ('This includes errors in: ...') adds no information beyond what the class name communicates
findings.suggestion: Collapse pass-only exception subclasses to a single-line docstring that states only what distinguishes this subclass from its parent. Remove Examples sections from trivial subclasses; they belong on the base class __init__ only.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/__init__.py re-exports 90+ symbols with no selection rationale, obscuring internal structure
evidence: infrastructure/core/__init__.py lines 14-246: __all__ contains 90+ entries spanning exceptions, logging, progress, retry, checkpoint, performance, health, security, config, environment, file ops, pipeline, multi-project, file inventory, pipeline summary
Symbols from 18 distinct sub-modules are all promoted to the same flat namespace with no grouping signal
The re-export surface includes implementation-detail types (ProfilingMetrics, StreamingProgress exported indirectly via logging_utils) alongside primary API entry points
findings.suggestion: Restrict __init__.py re-exports to the 10-15 symbols most commonly needed by cross-module callers. Move specialist symbols (ProfilingMetrics, StagePerformanceTracker, SecurityViolation, etc.) to direct sub-module imports at their call sites.

findings.summary: Logging implementation is split across 5 files that all live flat in infrastructure/core/ rather than a logging/ sub-package
evidence: infrastructure/core/ contains 26 files (8,947 LOC); 5 of them are logging_*.py sub-modules

logging_constants.py docstring line 1: 'Shared logging constants with no infrastructure imports' — explicitly exists to avoid circular imports within core/, which is a smell that the group has outgrown its directory
logging_utils.py imports from logging_formatters, logging_helpers, logging_progress, and logging_constants — a 4-level internal fan-out inside one package
The 5 logging files total ~1,200+ LOC and represent a cohesive sub-domain that would be better contained as infrastructure/core/logging/
findings.suggestion: Move logging_utils.py, logging_formatters.py, logging_helpers.py, logging_progress.py, and logging_constants.py into infrastructure/core/logging/ with a __init__.py that re-exports get_logger and the primary public API. This eliminates the circular-import workaround and reduces infrastructure/core/ from 26 to 22 files.

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/ contains test_reporter.py and test_summary_generator.py — names that signal test tooling living in a production package
evidence: infrastructure/reporting/ directory profile shows 14 files with 2 marked as 'test' zone: test_reporter.py and test_summary_generator.py

Both files are imported by scripts/ (12 imports from reporting/) and tests/infra_tests/reporting/ (14 imports), suggesting they are production utilities that generate test-related reports — but their names are indistinguishable from test files
test_summary_generator.py line 361: run_test_summary_generation() is a main entry point called from scripts, not a test utility — the 'test_' prefix is misleading in a production module
findings.suggestion: Rename test_reporter.py to coverage_reporter.py (or report_generator.py) and test_summary_generator.py to suite_summary_generator.py to clearly signal these are production report generators, not test files. Update all import references accordingly.

findings.summary: projects/cognitive_case_diagrams/src/visualization/styles.py is imported by 3 production files but has no test coverage
evidence: blind packet testing.critical_untested: styles.py listed with importers=3, meaning 3 production files depend on it
No test file targeting styles.py found in tests/infra_tests/ or projects/cognitive_case_diagrams/tests/
The visualization/ package has 6 files and 2,527 LOC — styles.py is the only untested file in this domain
findings.suggestion: Add a test file projects/cognitive_case_diagrams/tests/test_styles.py that instantiates style objects and validates their properties with real data. This closes the only identified coverage gap in an otherwise well-tested codebase.

findings.summary: Two parallel monitoring subsystems (PerformanceMonitor and CodeProfiler) coexist in performance.py with overlapping responsibilities and separate global singletons, blurring ownership of 'what monitors performance'
evidence: performance.py:76-148: PerformanceMonitor tracks resource usage via psutil with start/stop lifecycle
performance.py:368-471: CodeProfiler tracks the same concerns (timing, memory, function benchmarking) via cProfile/tracemalloc with its own history list
performance.py:513-521: get_performance_monitor() returns a global CodeProfiler singleton
performance.py:288-296: _get_checker() returns a separate global SystemHealthChecker singleton
performance.py:573-577: Module-level benchmark_function() creates a throwaway CodeProfiler() rather than using the global — inconsistent with the singleton pattern above
findings.suggestion: Merge PerformanceMonitor and CodeProfiler into a single class with a clear sub-domain split, or promote them to separate modules with distinct ownership boundaries. Eliminate the inconsistent throwaway instantiation in the module-level benchmark_function.

findings.summary: SystemHealthChecker._check_filesystem() constructs critical directory paths relative to cwd at call time, making the module's contract fragile and undocumented
evidence: health_check.py:98-104: Path('projects') and Path('infrastructure') are constructed without anchoring to any configured root — correctness depends on the process working directory

health_check.py:288-311: Module-level singletons _get_checker(), quick_health_check(), get_health_status(), get_health_metrics() all delegate to a single cached instance created at first call, baking in whatever cwd existed at that moment
findings.suggestion: Accept a repo_root parameter (Path) in SystemHealthChecker.__init__ and thread it through _check_filesystem, matching the pattern used by PipelineExecutor and CheckpointManager. Module-level convenience functions should require or infer repo_root rather than relying on cwd.

findings.summary: query_short() and query_long() compose behavior by prepending a plain-text instruction string and calling self.query(), conflating prompt construction with response-mode configuration and layering context effects unpredictably
evidence: client.py:398-403: query_short() prepends 'Provide a concise...' instruction then calls self.query(), which adds the concatenated string to context as a user message
client.py:453-458: query_long() does the same with a different instruction prefix
client.py:223: query() unconditionally calls self.context.add_message('user', prompt) — so the instruction prefix leaks into conversation history
client.py:392-396: GenerationOptions.max_tokens is correctly set for the mode, but the instruction injection happens separately, creating two distinct seams for the same purpose
findings.suggestion: Separate token-budget configuration (GenerationOptions) from prompt construction. Delegate to _generate_response_direct() directly for short/long modes with the appropriate options, or build a private _query_with_mode() that keeps instruction injection and context management in one coherent path.

findings.summary: load_infrastructure_results() tries three separate file paths with different field-extraction logic at each level, producing dicts with different key coverage at each fallback, making callers unable to reliably predict what fields are present
evidence: test_summary_generator.py:55-77: Primary path (infrastructure_validation_report.json) extracts 9 fields including 'passed', 'failed', 'duration_seconds', 'exit_code'
test_summary_generator.py:82-95: First fallback (coverage_infra.json) extracts only 4 fields: 'coverage_percent', 'total_lines', 'covered_lines', 'missing_lines' — 'passed', 'exit_code' are absent
test_summary_generator.py:98-111: Second fallback (htmlcov/coverage.json) extracts the same 4 fields as first fallback
test_summary_generator.py:187: generate_summary_report() calls infra_results.get('exit_code', 1) — defaults to failure when the fallback paths are used, despite coverage data being valid
findings.suggestion: Define a typed dataclass or TypedDict for InfraResults with a fixed field set and default values. Each loader path should return a fully populated instance (with sentinel/zero values for unavailable fields) so callers always see the same shape.

findings.summary: The performance_context() context manager calls monitor.stop() in its finally block but assigns the result to a local variable that is never returned or yielded, making the stop() call's metrics permanently inaccessible to callers
evidence: performance.py:151-164: @contextmanager yields monitor (started), then in finally: metrics = monitor.stop() followed by logger.debug(...) — metrics is a local variable that goes out of scope immediately
performance.py:159: yield monitor — callers receive the started monitor, but calling monitor.stop() themselves after the block would raise since the finally block already called stop()

The docstring (performance.py:155-157) says 'Yields the started PerformanceMonitor. Calls monitor.stop() in the finally block so callers can read PerformanceMetrics after exit' — but callers cannot read them since the return value is discarded
findings.suggestion: Either store metrics on the monitor instance before exiting (e.g., monitor.last_metrics = metrics) so callers can read it after the with-block, or restructure as a two-phase API. The current contract documented in the docstring does not match the implementation.

findings.summary: StagePerformanceTracker.get_performance_warnings() iterates self.stages in three separate loops for slow, high-memory, and high-cpu checks, where a single pass would suffice
evidence: performance.py:269-280: First for loop over self.stages checks duration > avg_duration * 2
performance.py:282-292: Second for loop over self.stages checks peak_memory_mb > 1024
performance.py:294-304: Third for loop over self.stages checks cpu_percent > 90
All three append to the same warnings list and share no interdependence
findings.suggestion: Merge into a single loop body that checks all three conditions per stage, avoiding repeated iteration over the same list.

findings.summary: Every public query method in LLMClient emits at least two structured debug log calls (start + complete) with overlapping field sets, creating significant log volume for routine operations
evidence: client.py:214-221: query() logs 'Starting query' with model, prompt_length
client.py:231-238: query() logs 'Query completed' with model, response_length, generation_time
client.py:305-315: query_raw() logs 'Starting raw query' with model, prompt_length, prompt_preview, add_to_context, max_tokens, temperature
client.py:324-332: query_raw() logs 'Raw query completed' with model, response_length, response_tokens_est, generation_time
client.py:381-389: query_short() adds a third start log before delegating to query() which logs its own start — yielding 4 log entries for one query_short() call
client.py:404-412: query_short() adds a fourth completion log after query() already logged completion
findings.suggestion: Log at the _generate_response_direct() boundary only (the single point of real I/O), removing the redundant entry/exit logs in the delegating query methods. Retain structured logging where fields carry diagnostics not captured by the lower level.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/performance.py exports two distinct flat APIs — one for psutil-based stage monitoring (get_system_resources, monitor_performance decorator, benchmark_llm_query, profile_memory_usage, benchmark_function) and one for CodeProfiler class usage — without a coherent organizing principle, making the right entry point for any given task non-obvious
evidence: performance.py:516-521: get_performance_monitor() exposes a global CodeProfiler for class-based use
performance.py:524-542: monitor_performance() decorator wraps CodeProfiler.monitor() via the global

performance.py:545-550: benchmark_llm_query() is a one-off convenience wrapping CodeProfiler.benchmark_function() for a single use-case (LLM queries), leaking a domain concern into a generic infrastructure module
performance.py:553-570: profile_memory_usage() reimplements tracemalloc logic that is already inside CodeProfiler.monitor(), bypassing the class entirely
performance.py:573-577: benchmark_function() creates a fresh throwaway CodeProfiler() rather than delegating to the global, inconsistent with monitor_performance() above
findings.suggestion: Separate stage-level monitoring (PerformanceMonitor, StagePerformanceTracker, performance_context) from function-level profiling (CodeProfiler). Remove benchmark_llm_query from this module — it belongs in the llm module. Consolidate profile_memory_usage and benchmark_function to delegate to CodeProfiler rather than re-implementing.

findings.summary: The is_infra boolean parameter threads through multiple private functions in coverage_parser.py to toggle debug logging, creating a quasi-configuration parameter that changes observable behavior rather than a simple switch
evidence: coverage_parser.py:233: _wait_for_coverage_file(paths, max_retries, delay, is_infra=False) — is_infra controls whether debug lines are emitted at lines 242, 248, 252, 253
coverage_parser.py:257: _parse_coverage_json(path, is_infra=False) — is_infra controls debug lines at 264, 267, 268
coverage_parser.py:276: extract_coverage_percentage(stdout_text, coverage_json_paths, is_infra=False) — propagates is_infra to both private functions
The only functional difference is whether logger.debug() is called — the computation is identical in both branches
findings.suggestion: Remove is_infra from all private function signatures. Use the module logger at DEBUG level unconditionally; controlling verbosity is the logger's responsibility. If context labeling is needed, pass a label string (e.g., suite_name='infra') rather than a boolean.
